examples.title: Three Schema Architecture - Basic Structure
examples.description: Demonstrates the three levels of database schema (External, Conceptual, Internal) and their separation
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the three-schema architecture: 1) Internal Schema handles physical storage, 2) Conceptual Schema defines logical structure and relationships, 3) External Schema provides user-specific views. Each level is independent - users see their view without knowing physical storage details.

examples.title: Data Independence Demonstration
examples.description: Shows how changes at one schema level don't affect other levels - the key benefit of three-schema architecture
examples.code:
# Data Independence Demonstration
# Changes at one level don't affect other levels

class DatabaseSystem:
    """Simulates a DBMS with data independence"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.internal_schema = {'storage_type': 'heap_file', 'index': 'B-tree'}
        self.conceptual_schema = {'tables': {'students': 'id, name, age'}}
        self.external_schemas = {
            'admin_view': {'students': 'id, name, age, salary'},
            'student_view': {'students': 'id, name, age'}
        }
    
    # Logical Data Independence: Changing conceptual doesn't affect external
    def update_conceptual_schema(self, new_schema):
        print("🔄 Updating Conceptual Schema...")
        old_schema = self.conceptual_schema.copy()
        self.conceptual_schema = new_schema
        print(f"Old: {old_schema}")
        print(f"New: {self.conceptual_schema}")
        print("✅ External views remain unchanged - Logical Data Independence achieved!")
        return self.external_schemas
    
    # Physical Data Independence: Changing internal doesn't affect conceptual
    def update_internal_schema(self, new_storage):
        print("🔄 Updating Internal Schema (Physical Storage)...")
        old_storage = self.internal_schema.copy()
        self.internal_schema = new_storage
        print(f"Old: {old_storage}")
        print(f"New: {self.internal_schema}")
        print("✅ Conceptual schema unchanged - Physical Data Independence achieved!")
        return self.conceptual_schema

# Demonstration
db = DatabaseSystem()

print("=== Initial State ===")
print(f"Internal: {db.internal_schema}")
print(f"Conceptual: {db.conceptual_schema}")
print(f"External: {db.external_schemas}")
print()

# Scenario 1: Change storage technology (Physical Data Independence)
print("--- Scenario 1: Upgrade Storage from HDD to SSD ---")
db.update_internal_schema({'storage_type': 'SSD', 'index': 'Hash'})
print()

# Scenario 2: Add new field to logical schema (Logical Data Independence)
print("--- Scenario 2: Add 'email' field to Students table ---")
db.update_conceptual_schema({'tables': {'students': 'id, name, age, email'}})
print()

print("=== Key Benefit ===")
print("Applications using external views continue to work without modification!")
examples.explanation: This example shows data independence in action: 1) Physical data independence - changing storage (HDD to SSD) doesn't affect the conceptual schema or applications, 2) Logical data independence - adding fields to tables doesn't break existing user views. This is the core benefit of the three-schema architecture.

examples.title: Schema Mapping and Transformation
examples.description: Advanced example showing how data flows between schemas with transformation/mapping
examples.explanation: This advanced example shows the complete data flow: 1) External to Conceptual - user data mapped to logical structure, 2) Conceptual to Internal - logical data transformed to physical storage format. The SchemaMapper class handles all transformations, demonstrating how the three-schema architecture provides abstraction and independence between levels.
